z The attempt of the council last
might to sneak out franchise
granting to the Northern Pacific
railroad the right to build 8 spurs
on Riverside av. east of its freight
yards was headed off by Council
man Dalke, who brought to the at
tention Of tie other councilmen the
fact that there was a long list of
protests to be considered.


Councilman Brown first asked
for the franchise which had been
on file, he probably thought. long
enough for the protests to be for
gotten. Dalke speedily dissipated
such erroneous views.


Attorney Edward ]. Cannon ap-
peared for the railroad company
and had DR.. E. E. Pitwood with
him as AFFIDAVIT man" Cannon
said all the protestants had been
appeased and now wanted the spur.
He referred to of them as DR..
Pittwood, who would tell the coun-
Gil about it. The clerk was asked
to read the list of those on the pro-
test. DR.. Pittwood's name was not
among them. Pittwood explained
that he was erecting an expensive
brick building in the vicinity of the
spurs and wanted to know If he
was to build for warehouse of
lodging house. it being contingent
upon whether or not the spur
tracks were to be built


The matter was referred to the
board of public works with instruc-
ton to ascertain the grade pos
sibilities and if the spurs would in
terfere with the grade of the rest
Of the street.
